verification_steps: doctor.inspectMeta (services/doctor.ts:322) calls tryBuildRuleMeta which re-hashes every .fabric/rules/*.md
buildRuleMeta computes new revision hash (rule-meta-builder.ts:64-66) from sorted node hashes
createMetaCheck (doctor.ts:540-557) compares stored meta.revision vs computedRevision; emits agents_meta_stale (fixable_error)
doctor --fix calls writeRuleMeta which atomicWrites agents.meta.json + rule-test.index.json AND appends 3 events to events.jsonl: rule_drift_detected, rule_baseline_accepted, baseline_synced
After --fix runs, rule-meta-builder.ts:281 invalidates contextCache via the doctor.ts:281 contextCache.invalidate('meta_write', projectRoot)
gaps: fab serve does NOT watch .fabric/rules/*.md (http.ts:167-177 only watches .fabric/agents.meta.json + .fabric/bootstrap/README.md). Editing a rule file with serve running produces NO file_watch invalidation, NO tool/list_changed notification, NO resource_updated notification. Stale rule content can sit in 5-second cache.
fab scan (cli/commands/scan.ts) does NOT inspect .fabric/rules/ at all — it scans the user project for framework signals only. There is no equivalent of `fabric scan --rules`.
Doctor only re-hashes when the user runs the command. There is no background reconciliation.

detection_pipeline.trigger: edit .fabric/rules/<id>.md to change <!-- fab:rule-id NEW_ID --> declared stable_id
verification_steps: deriveRuleIdentity (rule-meta-builder.ts:680-706) reads the new declared stable_id and writes it to nodes[id].stable_id
computeRevision (rule-meta-builder.ts:566-571) hashes [id|hash|stable_id|identity_source] so a stable_id change DOES trigger a revision change → agents_meta_stale
rule-test.index.json links keyed by old stable_id become orphan_annotations after writeRuleMeta runs
gaps: There is no validation that the new stable_id is unique. Two rules declaring the same `<!-- fab:rule-id duplicate -->` would silently overwrite in indexRulesByStableId (rule-meta-builder.ts:372-382 — Map.set wins, last-write keeps).

stable_id format is enforced only by regex in extractDeclaredStableId (rule-meta-builder.ts:710 — /[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9/_-]*/). An invalid id is silently ignored (returns undefined), then derived stable_id is used WITHOUT a doctor warning that the user's intended id was rejected.

@fabric-verify annotations in test files (key-by string) are NEVER validated to point at actual stable_ids until they get re-indexed; until then they live as orphan_annotations forever with no doctor surface.

detection_pipeline.trigger: edit .fabric/agents.meta.json description field manually (drift from rule .md frontmatter)
verification_steps: computeRulesBasedAgentsMeta (rule-meta-builder.ts:128-147) re-extracts description via extractRuleDescription from the .md source on each build
stableStringify comparison at rule-meta-builder.ts:71-72 will detect the drift
Result: agents_meta_stale (fixable). doctor --fix overwrites the manual description with the .md-derived one
gaps: User has no warning that their hand-edited description will be silently nuked on next doctor --fix. The agents.meta.json description field is effectively REGENERATED ALWAYS — but doctor's stale-message just says 'revision X does not match Y' without naming WHICH fields drifted.
If a user wants to override the description, they must edit the rule .md frontmatter (there is no 'authoritative meta' mode).

detection_pipeline.trigger: user manually creates .fabric/rules/foo/bar.md without running any tool
verification_steps: buildRuleMeta finds the new file via findFabricRuleFiles (rule-meta-builder.ts:277-306)
A new node is added with derived stable_id; revision changes; agents_meta_stale fires
After doctor --fix, agents.meta.json now has the node and fab_plan_context can return it
gaps: BEFORE doctor --fix runs, fab_plan_context (services/plan-context.ts:218 — Object.entries(meta.nodes)) and get-rules (services/get-rules.ts:157) iterate ONLY agents.meta.json.nodes. So a manually-added rule is INVISIBLE to AI agents until doctor --fix is run. There is no auto-discovery.
fab serve's chokidar at http.ts:167 does not watch .fabric/rules/, so even with the daemon running the new rule does not trigger any cache invalidation or tools/list_changed notification.
No CLI command surfaces 'unindexed rules detected' to the user proactively.

verification_steps: buildRuleMeta no longer sees the file → node disappears from computed meta
But createRuleContentRefCheck (doctor.ts:559-585) inspects ONLY the existing meta.json's content_refs. If meta still has the entry, content_ref_missing fires (manual_error, NOT fixable).
doctor --fix on stale meta would, via writeRuleMeta, drop the node and write the new revision. Indirect cleanup.
gaps: content_ref_missing is classified manual_error (doctor.ts:574-581) but the fix is in fact deterministic: regenerate meta. User is told to manually fix what the system could fix automatically.

The dangling rule-test.index.json links pointing at the deleted stable_id become orphan_annotations after doctor --fix. There is no cleanup ledger event for 'rule_deleted' — only baseline_synced gets logged.
If a user deletes ALL rules, agents.meta.json shrinks to just L0 — no warning that 'this empty meta means AI gets only L0 context.'

verification_steps: deriveAgentsMetaStableId is computed from path. Renaming the file changes the derived stable_id.
buildRuleMeta inserts a NEW node (new id, new derived stable_id) and the old one disappears. revision changes.
gaps: All @fabric-verify test annotations pinning the old stable_id become orphan_annotations silently (no error, no warning code).

If users want to preserve identity across renames, they MUST add an explicit `<!-- fab:rule-id stable -->` BEFORE renaming. This contract is documented only in rule-meta-builder.ts source code (identity_source: 'declared' branch at L684-689) and not surfaced anywhere in the CLI / docs / wizard.

rule_change_review.stable_id_change_handling: Edit-and-pray. A stable_id change (declared via `<!-- fab:rule-id ... -->` in the .md) is detected at write-time (next doctor or doctor --fix run) only as a 'revision drift'. The doctor message does NOT distinguish 'a hash drifted' from 'a stable_id changed' — they both surface as agents_meta_stale. Downstream consumers (rule-test.index.json links, @fabric-verify annotations in test files) that referenced the old stable_id silently become orphan_annotations after the next writeRuleMeta. There is no symmetric 'old_stable_ids' carryover; once the rebuild happens the old id is gone from meta and the only trace is in events.jsonl rule_drift_detected/baseline_synced events.
rule_change_review.meta_rule_drift_handling: agents.meta.json is REGENERATED FROM SCRATCH on every writeRuleMeta call (rule-meta-builder.ts:128-153). Description, sections, hash, content_ref are ALL recomputed from the .md source. Hand-edits to agents.meta.json are silently overwritten on the next doctor --fix. The only fields that survive are those merged via `...existing?.node` (rule-meta-builder.ts:138) — which currently means: priority, deps, scope_glob, layer, topology_type, level, stable_id (only when identity_source === 'declared' AND existing was declared). No doctor check warns the user 'your manual edits to agents.meta.json will be regenerated.' Users who treat agents.meta.json as authoritative get bitten on first doctor --fix.
rule_change_review.manual_rule_addition_discoverability: Invisible to fab_plan_context until doctor --fix runs. Both plan-context.ts:218 and get-rules.ts:157 iterate Object.entries(meta.nodes) — meaning an unindexed rule file under .fabric/rules/ never reaches an AI client. The chokidar watcher in http.ts:167 watches only agents.meta.json + bootstrap README, NOT .fabric/rules/, so even a long-running serve does not auto-invalidate when a new rule file appears. There is also no scan-time signal: scan command (cli/commands/scan.ts) does not inspect .fabric/rules/. The de facto contract is: 'the AI must call fabric doctor --fix between adding a rule and using it.' This contract is not stated in user docs.
rule_change_review.deletion_dangling_refs: Two-way drift. (1) Removing a rule file leaves agents.meta.json with a content_ref pointing at a missing path → createRuleContentRefCheck emits content_ref_missing as MANUAL_ERROR even though doctor.ts:255 could trivially regenerate the meta to drop the entry. The user is told to fix manually but doctor --fix actually does fix it (via the rule_meta rebuild path) — except content_ref_missing is NOT in the fixable_error code list at doctor.ts:271. There is a real bug: content_ref_missing is auto-recoverable but not auto-fixed. (2) rule-test.index.json links pointing at the now-missing rule become orphan_annotations after writeRuleMeta runs, with no surface in doctor (no orphan_annotations_present check exists). The cumulative orphans list grows over time with zero visibility.

pain_points.scenario: User edits a rule file while `fab serve` is running, expects AI to immediately see the change
pain_points.mental_model: Live-reload — hot-reload semantics, edit and refresh
pain_points.reality: chokidar at http.ts:167 watches only agents.meta.json + bootstrap README. Rule .md edits go undetected until doctor --fix runs and rewrites agents.meta.json. AI agents continue serving 5-second-cached stale content.
pain_points.fix_idea: Extend cacheWatcher pattern at http.ts:167 to include `.fabric/rules/**/*.md`. On change, contextCache.invalidate('file_watch', projectRoot) PLUS a debounced 'doctor staleness hint' notification (just an info-level log to stdout — not a hard re-meta build, since rebuild requires writing). Cost: ~15 LOC. R-code: defer to v1.8.0 batch alongside R3 signal handlers.

pain_points.scenario: User adds .fabric/rules/security/jwt.md by hand; asks Claude/Codex to use it
pain_points.mental_model: If I drop a rule file in .fabric/rules/, the AI sees it
pain_points.reality: fab_plan_context iterates meta.nodes only. Until doctor --fix is run the new rule is invisible. AI returns 'rule not found' or silently ignores.
pain_points.fix_idea: Add a doctor warning code `rules_dir_unindexed` that lists .md files under .fabric/rules/ NOT present as nodes in agents.meta.json. Trivial extension of rule-meta-builder findings. Make it auto-fixable via the existing writeRuleMeta path. Doctor message: 'N rule files in .fabric/rules/ are not indexed in agents.meta.json. Run `fab doctor --fix` to index them.'

pain_points.scenario: User edits agents.meta.json by hand to override a rule's description field
pain_points.mental_model: agents.meta.json is the authoritative metadata I can edit
pain_points.reality: Description is regenerated from .md frontmatter on every doctor --fix. User edit is silently nuked. No warning. Symptom: 'why did my description change back?'
pain_points.fix_idea: Either (a) add a doctor INFO check `agents_meta_user_edited` that warns 'description fields in agents.meta.json are derived from rule .md frontmatter; edits will be overwritten — use frontmatter `description:` instead', OR (b) document explicitly in initialization.md that agents.meta.json is a derived artifact like rule-test.index.json. Recommend (b) for v1.7.1.

pain_points.mental_model: Rename is a path change, identity is the file's purpose
pain_points.reality: Derived stable_id changes from `auth` to `security/auth`. All @fabric-verify annotations in test files become orphan_annotations after writeRuleMeta. No CLI command surfaces this; the user only finds out when a downstream consumer fails or by reading rule-test.index.json directly.
pain_points.fix_idea: Add a doctor warning `rule_test_orphans_present` (informational) when rule-test.index.json.orphan_annotations.length > 0. Currently they exist silently. Cost: <20 LOC, fits the same pattern as createRuleTestIndexCheck. Defer the actual fix (cross-rename detection) — the warning alone gives users a signal that something they did broke a link.

pain_points.mental_model: Deleting a rule should clean up its meta entry
pain_points.reality: doctor reports content_ref_missing as manual_error. User is told to manually fix what doctor --fix would actually fix (regen meta drops the node). Frustrating: manual_error is misleading.
pain_points.fix_idea: Reclassify content_ref_missing from manual_error to fixable_error and route it through the existing writeRuleMeta path in runDoctorFix (doctor.ts:270-282). One-line change to the issue list AND createRuleContentRefCheck status mapping. Cost: ~10 LOC. Defer to v1.8.0 batch.

pain_points.scenario: Two rules accidentally declare the same `<!-- fab:rule-id duplicate -->`
pain_points.mental_model: Duplicate IDs would be a hard error
pain_points.reality: indexRulesByStableId (rule-meta-builder.ts:372-382) silently overwrites — last sort-order wins. No doctor check surfaces duplicates. Downstream: @fabric-verify annotations may resolve to the wrong rule, or rule-test.index.json links point at one of the duplicates non-deterministically.
pain_points.fix_idea: Add doctor manual_error `stable_id_collision` that detects duplicate stable_ids during inspectMeta. Print all colliding files. Block doctor --fix until resolved (manual_error blocks the green-light state). Cost: ~25 LOC. Defer to v1.8.0; significant correctness fix.

pain_points.scenario: User declares `<!-- fab:rule-id 123-bad-id -->` (starts with digit OR uses unsupported chars)
pain_points.mental_model: The system will tell me my id is invalid
pain_points.reality: extractDeclaredStableId regex requires `[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9/_-]*` — first char must be alphanumeric. If invalid, regex returns undefined, code falls back to derived stable_id silently. User believes they declared an id but the system uses a derived one.
pain_points.fix_idea: On invalid declared id, append a doctor warning `rule_declared_id_invalid` that names the file and the rejected pattern. Continue using derived id but make the rejection visible. Cost: ~15 LOC.

stages.name: stage 0: preflight + intent resolution
outputs: InitCliIntent: { target, options, mcpInstallMode, interactiveSummary, wizardEnabled }
invariants: target must exist as directory (assertExistingDirectory at L1213)
force or reapply allows overwrite of existing artifacts; otherwise planFreshPath throws cli.init.errors.abort-existing

stages.name: stage 1: scaffold (fabric-managed files)
outputs: .fabric/agents.meta.json (STUB L0 ONLY: nodes={L0:{...}}; no rules yet)
invariants: agents.meta.json scaffold is INTENTIONALLY a stub — only L0 node, revision = sha256(L0 hash), no rules. The CLI does NOT call buildRuleMeta during init.
events.jsonl is created EMPTY — no init event written by the CLI. First event ledger entry only happens later when an AI skill invokes writeRuleMeta.
All file writes use writeFileSync (synchronous, NON-atomic). Mid-init crash leaves partial state.

rmSync(plan.fabricDir) at L562-564 only fires if plan.replaceFabricDir is true — i.e., when .fabric exists as a FILE, not a directory. With --reapply on existing init, .fabric/rules/ is NOT wiped; existing user rules survive.
error_modes: template not found (cli.shared.template-not-found in findTemplatePath at L1349-1363) — irrecoverable without reinstall
EPERM/EACCES on writeFileSync — irrecoverable per file
JSON parse failure on existing .claude/settings.json — gracefully degrades to skipped-invalid (init.ts:946-953) but the user's claude Stop-hook merge silently fails

stages.name: stage 2: bootstrap (cross-client install)
outputs: per-client bootstrap markdown copied to client-native locations (.cursor/, .codex/, .claude/, etc.)
invariants: resolveClients returns writers ONLY for detected clients (existsSync(<client_dir>) check). Undetected clients are silently skipped.
Skipping ALL clients yields cli.bootstrap.install.no-targets warning but is NOT an error.
error_modes: per-client copy failure caught at executeInitStagePlan L820-823 → stage disposition='failed', other stages continue. Failure REASON is printed to stderr but NOT structured into result.

stages.name: stage 3: mcp (per-client MCP server config)
outputs: ~/.claude/settings.json mcpServers update (NONCOMPLIANT: should be ~/.claude.json or .mcp.json — captured in architectural.json R5)
invariants: Local-mode install adds the dependency BEFORE writing client configs. If npm install fails the MCP config files reference a path that does not yet exist.
error_modes: pnpm/npm install failure (caught in stage failure block) — leaves Claude/Cursor pointing at a missing node_modules path
TOML upsert failure — caught and propagated

stages.name: stage 4: hooks (husky pre-commit hook)
invariants: No hard dependency on prior stages — hooks are independent.
error_modes: husky install failure caught at executeInitStagePlan

stages.name: stage 5 (HANDOFF): AI-driven taxonomy + rules generation
inputs: user interview answers (Phase 1: framework confirm; Phase 2: invariants extraction; Phase 3: rules construction)
outputs: .fabric/init-context.json (NEW — written by AI, NOT by CLI)
agents.meta.json updated (AI is supposed to update node tree and revision hash)
invariants: Claude path: claude-skills/agents-md-init/SKILL.md prescribes the 3-phase workflow with explicit invariants extraction and rule writing. Codex path: codex-skills/fabric-init/SKILL.md is much thinner — only says 'summarize next initialization actions', does NOT prescribe writing init-context.json or generating rules.

Claude/Codex Stop hooks (claude-hooks/agents-md-init-reminder.cjs L8-18; codex-hooks/fabric-stop-reminder.cjs) BLOCK the AI from declaring 'done' when forensic.json exists but init-context.json does not. This is the only enforcement gate.
agents.meta.json revision integrity is the AI's responsibility — there is no schema check that the AI's update produces a hash chain consistent with the rule files it wrote.
error_modes: AI ignores or partially completes the skill → init-context.json never written → doctor's createInitContextCheck (doctor.ts:530-538) reports init_context_missing as MANUAL_ERROR forever; doctor exit code is 1 forever; doctor --fix does NOT auto-fix this.

AI writes init-context.json but generates no rule files → agents.meta.json stays at the L0-only stub. fab_plan_context returns only L0 context. AI effectively has no project-specific rules until the user manually writes rules and runs doctor --fix.
AI writes rule files BUT does not update agents.meta.json → next doctor flags agents_meta_stale (recoverable via doctor --fix).
Codex skill is too thin to drive the full handoff → Codex users may end up in a different / under-initialized state vs Claude users.

seams.to: stage 5 AI skill (Claude agents-md-init / Codex fabric-init)
seams.contract: Implicit. The AI skill consumes forensic.json keys: framework, topology.by_ext, entry_points, recommendations_for_skill. The AI skill consumes INITIAL_TAXONOMY.md as text context. There is NO formal schema versioning between the CLI's forensic output and the SKILL's expected input.
seams.drift_risk: If the CLI changes forensic.json shape (renames a field, adds a section the SKILL doesn't expect), the SKILL silently produces wrong invariants. There is no test asserting forensic.json fields the SKILL templates rely on remain stable. The protected_tokens list in shared/i18n DOES protect literal strings 'AGENTS.md', '.fabric/forensic.json' but does not protect FIELD NAMES inside forensic.json.
seams.pain_point: Roundtripping a SKILL update requires republishing CLI templates (the SKILL is a copyfile from packages/cli/templates/ at init time). A user who initialized 6 months ago has a stale skill in .claude/skills/agents-md-init/SKILL.md. There is no doctor check 'skill_template_outdated'.

seams.from: stage 1 scaffold writes agents.meta.json STUB (only L0 node)
seams.to: stage 5 AI skill SHOULD update agents.meta.json with L1/L2 nodes after writing rule files
seams.contract: Both CLI and SKILL share the agentsMetaSchema (packages/shared) — but only the SKILL prose says 'update revision hash'. Nothing in the CLI sets up the AI for using rule-meta-builder.writeRuleMeta — the AI is implicitly expected to write meta by hand OR call MCP somehow.
seams.drift_risk: The AI may hand-write a meta.json with a wrong revision hash (it cannot easily compute sha256 of sorted rule entries). On next doctor --fix, the meta is regenerated from the .md files and the AI's hash is replaced. If the AI also wrote stable_ids that don't match derived stable_ids, those are kept (identity_source=declared) — but if the AI wrote them WITHOUT the `<!-- fab:rule-id -->` comment in the source .md, the next writeRuleMeta wipes them.
seams.pain_point: The right user flow is 'AI writes rule files → AI tells user to run doctor --fix'. But the SKILL.md prescribes 'AI updates agents.meta.json directly' (skill L43-46). These two flows produce different end-states. Sound only if the AI has run the full computeRevision logic — which it can't reliably do.

seams.contract: events.jsonl format is JSONL of eventLedgerEventSchema (shared). Events are append-only. CLI does NOT write any 'init_completed' event during scaffold.
seams.drift_risk: Low — schema is shared; init is a no-op writer of this file.
seams.pain_point: There is no 'init_completed' event in the ledger, so a downstream consumer cannot reconstruct 'when did this project finish init?' from the ledger alone — it must look at .fabric/forensic.json.generated_at. Minor.

seams.contract: init merges Fabric's Stop hook into existing settings.hooks.Stop array. If settings.json is invalid JSON or hooks is non-record, init returns 'skipped-invalid' and writes nothing.
seams.drift_risk: Medium. (1) settings.json is the WRONG file for MCP config per Claude Code spec (architectural.json R5) — Fabric is leaning on a path that may be removed by Claude Code in a future version. (2) Two parallel CLAUDE installs (a different tool ALSO writes Stop hooks) → array can grow unbounded with duplicate Fabric hooks, init has no de-dup.
seams.pain_point: init silently skips the merge if user's settings.json has any structural issue, with only a stderr log. The user would notice only by reading stderr or running doctor on Claude install (which Fabric does not check).

seams.from: init's pre-existing root-level CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md / .windsurfrules check
seams.contract: NONE. init does not check for, ingest, warn about, or migrate any pre-existing root-level CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or .windsurfrules. (Verified: zero references in init.ts.)
seams.drift_risk: High for migration-from-other-tools users. A user with an existing CLAUDE.md gets two truth sources: their pre-existing one (which their AI client still reads as system prompt) and Fabric's MCP-resource-served bootstrap README. There is no warning, no merge, no documentation about which wins.
seams.pain_point: Architectural.json correctly noted that Fabric SIDESTEPS the AGENTS.md drift problem by not writing those files itself. But the OTHER side of that — silent coexistence with user-existing files from prior tools — is unhandled. A migrating user thinks Fabric replaced their CLAUDE.md when in fact both are still active.

seams.contract: Files are expected to all-or-none exist after init returns. No transactional guarantee.
seams.drift_risk: Mid-init crash (Ctrl-C between L573 and L583) leaves partial state: bootstrap README written, agents.meta.json written, taxonomy written, rules dir created — but events.jsonl or forensic.json potentially missing. doctor would correctly detect missing files but the user has no clear 'init was interrupted, run --reapply' guidance.
seams.pain_point: init has no rollback / resume semantics. --reapply does what 'recover from partial init' should do but the relationship is not documented.

taxonomy_to_rules.description: INITIAL_TAXONOMY.md is a markdown summary of forensic findings (key_dirs as L1 buckets, candidate_files as L2 signals). It is NEVER mechanically converted into rules — it is a reading prompt for the AI skill. The actual taxonomy → rules step happens during stage 5 (AI-driven), where the AI interviews the user (Phase 1: framework confirm, Phase 2: invariants Q&A, Phase 3: write rule files). Output rule files have no structural guarantee that they match the L1 buckets named in INITIAL_TAXONOMY.md — the AI can deviate freely. There is no doctor check verifying that L1 rules cover the L1 buckets of INITIAL_TAXONOMY.md.

agents_md_init_handoff.explanation: Two-step flow embedded across (1) CLI scaffold stage that writes templates to .claude/skills/ and .agents/skills/ AND a Stop hook that blocks the AI until init-context.json exists, and (2) AI skill execution where the AI reads forensic.json + bootstrap README, interviews the user, then writes init-context.json + rule files + (optionally) updates agents.meta.json. Claude path is well-prescribed (3-phase workflow, ~150-line SKILL.md). Codex path (codex-skills/fabric-init/SKILL.md, ~25 lines) is much thinner — it does not prescribe writing init-context.json, generating rules, or updating agents.meta.json. Codex hook (codex-hooks/fabric-stop-reminder.cjs) checks the same gate but the underlying SKILL is incapable of satisfying it without ad-hoc AI interpretation. Cursor has NO equivalent skill or hook in the bootstrap output — Cursor users get stuck at the post-init handoff with no automation.
problem_areas: Codex skill content is dramatically thinner than Claude skill (verified: cli/templates/codex-skills/fabric-init/SKILL.md vs cli/templates/claude-skills/agents-md-init/SKILL.md). Codex users get a different handoff outcome and may finish init with no rules written.

Cursor: there is NO cursor-skill or cursor-hook in init's scaffold output (init.ts L494-501 emits Claude + Codex paths only). Cursor users receive bootstrap + MCP config but no hand-off automation. They must manually invoke the AI to do the agents-md-init flow without any reminder.

init-context.json is required by doctor (createInitContextCheck) but is written by an external AI process. doctor reports init_context_missing as MANUAL_ERROR with NO actionable hint pointing the user to the agents-md-init skill. Message: '.fabric/init-context.json is missing.' — does not say 'run the agents-md-init skill in your AI client'.

The agents.meta.json STUB written by init has revision = sha256(L0_hash) — a real value. After the AI writes rules, if the AI does not update agents.meta.json, doctor --fix WILL regenerate it, which produces a NEW revision and triggers rule_drift_detected event in events.jsonl with previous_revision = the L0-only stub. The first-ever drift event is therefore noise from the init-handoff itself, not from a real change.

gaps: init does NOT scan for or warn about pre-existing root-level CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .windsurfrules, .cursorrules, GEMINI.md (note: GEMINI.md is now part of the retired-clients set anyway). For migrating users these files continue to be read by the AI client as system prompts, in parallel with Fabric's MCP-served bootstrap. Two truth sources, no migration prompt.

init does check for pre-existing .claude/settings.json (L943-955) — but only for the JSON parse / hooks shape. Other pre-existing keys are preserved (passthrough merge on L985 at `{...hooks, Stop: [...] }`).
init does check for pre-existing .codex/hooks.json (L911-923) — if exists and !force/reapply, action='skipped'. Existing user hooks are preserved.
init does NOT check for pre-existing ~/.codex/config.toml [mcp_servers] entries beyond Fabric's own block — verified: toml.ts upsertCodexServerBlock only touches its own [mcp_servers.fabric] block.

init does NOT detect or warn about a pre-existing .fabric/ from another tool (e.g., a different fabric-named project) before overwriting — only `--reapply` / `--force` gate prevents the overwrite, and the gate's error message says 'abort-existing' without explaining 'this is YOUR previous Fabric init, not another tool.'

preserved: .fabric/rules/**/*.md (NEVER touched by executeInitFabricPlan — rmSync at L562-564 only fires when .fabric is a FILE, not a directory)
.fabric/init-context.json (init NEVER writes this; --reapply does not delete it)
user's existing .codex/hooks.json keys other than Fabric's (init merges, not overwrites)
user's existing .claude/settings.json keys other than hooks.Stop (init merges)
overwritten: .fabric/bootstrap/README.md (regenerated from framework template — may overwrite user customization)
.fabric/agents.meta.json (REGENERATED AS L0-ONLY STUB — destroys any rule nodes the AI wrote in stage 5; the user must run doctor --fix afterward to recover the rule index)
.fabric/INITIAL_TAXONOMY.md (regenerated from current forensic — may have shifted text)
.fabric/events.jsonl (TRUNCATED TO EMPTY — destroys all drift history!)
.codex/hooks.json (overwritten when force; original user customizations to non-Fabric hooks lost)
checked: Existence check (planFreshPath at L842-852) gates writes — but with --reapply force=true is implied, so all checks resolve to 'overwritten' silently.
No checksum / version comparison between existing scaffold files and what init would write — init does NOT skip a write if the existing file is bit-identical to the template.

pain_points.scenario: User runs `fab init --reapply` to refresh after a Fabric upgrade
pain_points.fix_idea: events.jsonl is TRUNCATED to empty by --reapply (planFreshPath returns 'overwritten' for eventsPath at L507; executeInitFabricPlan rewrites it empty at L580). All drift history is lost — including baseline_synced events that other tools may rely on. Recommend: --reapply should NOT truncate events.jsonl. Either skip if exists or rotate it to events.jsonl.bak with a one-line ledger entry 'init_reapplied'. Cost: ~10 LOC in executeInitFabricPlan.

pain_points.scenario: User runs `fab init --reapply` after AI has populated rules
pain_points.fix_idea: agents.meta.json is regenerated as the L0-only stub (createInitialMeta at L1256-1271). The user's stage-5 AI-written nodes vanish. doctor will then flag agents_meta_stale, but the experience is jarring: the user just ran `--reapply` to 'refresh,' and now they need a SECOND command (`doctor --fix`) to recover. Recommend: --reapply should call writeRuleMeta as a final step, OR skip writing agents.meta.json if .fabric/rules/ has any .md files. Cost: ~20 LOC.

pain_points.scenario: User runs `fab init` on a repo with existing CLAUDE.md from a prior tool
pain_points.fix_idea: init does not detect or surface this. AI gets two parallel system-prompt sources. Recommend: after stage 1, scan workspace root for [CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .cursorrules, .windsurfrules]. If any exist, print a stderr WARNING listing them with text 'Fabric uses MCP resources, not root-level files. These pre-existing files are still read by your AI client and may conflict with .fabric/bootstrap/README.md — consider migrating their content into rules under .fabric/rules/ and removing the originals.' Cost: ~30 LOC. Defer to v1.8.0.

pain_points.scenario: User runs `fab init` and the AI never executes the agents-md-init skill (skill ignored, network error, model issue)
pain_points.fix_idea: User is stuck with init-context.json missing forever. doctor reports manual_error with no action hint. Recommend: createInitContextCheck error message should include 'Run the `agents-md-init` skill in Claude Code, or `fabric-init` in Codex. Without it Fabric only has L0 context.' Cost: ~5 LOC i18n change.

pain_points.scenario: Cursor user finishes init (bootstrap + MCP only) and has no skill/hook to drive stage 5
pain_points.fix_idea: init does not emit a Cursor-specific skill or hook. Cursor users hit a dead-end at stage 5. Recommend (v1.8.0): EITHER (a) add a Cursor-equivalent skill template under templates/cursor-rules/agents-md-init.mdc and emit it during init, OR (b) document explicitly in initialization.md that Cursor users must manually copy the agents-md-init skill content into a Cursor rule. Cost: ~50 LOC + new template file. Out of stabilization scope but a real first-class-citizen gap for Cursor.

pain_points.scenario: Codex user follows codex-skills/fabric-init/SKILL.md and it does not produce init-context.json
pain_points.fix_idea: Codex skill is much thinner than Claude skill — it does not prescribe writing init-context.json. Codex users either bridge the gap by accident or get stuck. Recommend: bring codex-skills/fabric-init/SKILL.md to feature parity with claude-skills/agents-md-init/SKILL.md (replicate the 3-phase workflow). Cost: ~80 LOC of skill markdown. Defer to v1.8.0; high parity-ROI.

Synchronous writeFileSync sequence at executeInitFabricPlan L569-583 has no rollback. Some files are written, some are not. Doctor on the half-init project gives a confusing mix of present/missing checks. Recommend: print a final stderr 'Fabric init complete' line to stdout AFTER every scaffold write succeeds, AND have doctor's first paragraph say 'If an init was interrupted, run `fab init --reapply` to recover.' Cost: ~10 LOC + doctor output tweak.

pain_points.scenario: User clones a repo with existing .fabric/ from a previous Fabric version
pain_points.fix_idea: init aborts on existing files unless --reapply. The error message says 'abort-existing' but doesn't suggest --reapply. Recommend: error message should explicitly hint 'Use `fab init --reapply` to refresh existing files; use `fab doctor` to diagnose without writes.' Cost: ~5 LOC i18n.

interaction_pain_points.example: packages/cli/src/commands/init.ts:625-630 shouldUseInitWizard branches on isInteractiveInit() AND args.interactive AND !args.yes. With --yes, wizard is bypassed and the entire user-confirmation flow disappears — no preview, no confirmation, just execute. With non-TTY (CI), behavior is identical to --yes (silent execute). No preview-on-stderr happens unless --plan is passed.
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Compliant for the --yes case (explicit user opt-in to non-interactive). For non-TTY without --yes, current behavior silently degrades to --yes-equivalent. Recommend: when non-TTY AND no --yes AND no --plan, print a stderr WARNING 'No TTY detected; proceeding non-interactively. Use --plan to preview or --yes to suppress this warning.' Cost: ~10 LOC.

interaction_pain_points.example: packages/server/src/services/doctor.ts:498-624 every doctor check message is hardcoded English and lacks an action hint. createInitContextCheck (L530-538) says '.fabric/init-context.json is missing.' — does not say 'run agents-md-init skill in your AI client'. createTaxonomyCheck says '.fabric/INITIAL_TAXONOMY.md is missing.' — does not say 'run fab init --reapply'.
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Append actionable hints to every error message. Pattern: '<problem>. Recommended fix: <command or skill>.' Touch all 9 issueCheck callsites. Cost: ~40 LOC. Defer to v1.8.0; high-ROI for first-time users.

interaction_pain_points.example: packages/cli/src/commands/init.ts:820-823 catches stage failures and prints formatInitStageFailure, but the disposition is recorded as 'failed' without surfacing the cause structurally. The stderr reason vanishes from the InitExecutionResult; downstream callers (e.g., a CI script doing `fab init --json`) cannot tell WHY a stage failed.
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Capture the error message in InitStageRecord (extend the type to include `reason?: string`). Persist into result.stageResults. Cost: ~15 LOC in init.ts.

interaction_pain_points.example: packages/cli/src/commands/init.ts:1024-1031 and 1106-1113 — wizard asks 'continue with target?' AND 'execute confirm?'. Both default initialValue=true. With Enter-key spam users sail through. There is NO confirmation specifically gated on --reapply (which is destructive: wipes events.jsonl, regenerates agents.meta.json).
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Add a wizard step (only when options.reapply === true) that says 'WARNING: --reapply will overwrite .fabric/bootstrap/README.md, .fabric/agents.meta.json, .fabric/INITIAL_TAXONOMY.md, .fabric/forensic.json AND TRUNCATE .fabric/events.jsonl. Existing rule files are preserved. Continue?' default=false. Cost: ~20 LOC + i18n strings.

interaction_pain_points.example: User starts with Cursor; later adopts Codex. The natural action is `fab init --reapply` to add Codex configs. But --reapply destroys .fabric/events.jsonl AND regenerates agents.meta.json to L0-stub. There is no docs page covering 'how to add a client to an existing Fabric project'. docs/getting-started.md L46 mentions --reapply but only for 'reapply' purposes, not 'add a client'.
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Two parts. (1) `fab config install --clients=codex` already EXISTS as a non-destructive way to add a client's MCP config — but it skips bootstrap/hooks. Document this flow explicitly: 'To add a client to an existing project: `fab config install --clients=<new>` then `fab bootstrap install --clients=<new>`.' (2) Make --reapply less destructive (per init pain points above). Cost: docs ~30 lines + the init fixes already proposed.

interaction_pain_points.example: Fabric in repo A and repo B share NOTHING by default. ~/.codex/config.toml has [mcp_servers.fabric] block (single-server, last-write-wins). ~/.claude/settings.json has Stop hooks (merged additively across runs but the hook command is the SAME path /.claude/hooks/agents-md-init-reminder.cjs which is REPO-LOCAL, not user-global). Result: opening repo B with claude code DOES read repo-B's .claude/hooks/, so things work — but the global ~/.claude/settings.json points its Stop hook at REPO-A's path. Quietly inconsistent.
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Confirm whether ~/.claude/settings.json Stop hook should be repo-relative or absolute. Currently CLAUDE_INIT_REMINDER_COMMAND (init.ts:244) is '.claude/hooks/agents-md-init-reminder.cjs' — relative, which Claude Code resolves against cwd at runtime. If true, this is fine; if Claude resolves vs the settings.json location, it's broken. Recommend: add a vitest spec asserting Claude's settings.json gets a relative path AND a docs note. Cost: ~15 LOC.

interaction_pain_points.example: doctor --fix runs writeRuleMeta which silently appends 3 events to events.jsonl (rule_drift_detected, rule_baseline_accepted, baseline_synced) AND bumps the revision hash. The user sees 'Applied N deterministic doctor fixes.' but does NOT see WHICH revision they're now at, WHICH stable_ids changed, or that ledger entries were written. The data is in events.jsonl but the UI is silent.
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Append to runDoctorFix message: 'Revision: <prev> → <new>. Synced files: N. See .fabric/events.jsonl for details.' Cost: ~10 LOC in createFixMessage (doctor.ts:677-686).

interaction_pain_points.example: No bash/zsh/fish completion files in repo. citty (the CLI framework) supports completions but none are exported via package.json bin or generated artifacts. `fabric --help` and `fab --help` work but lack auto-complete for subcommands and --clients=cursor,codex.
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: citty has built-in completion generation via runMain. Wire a `fabric completion <shell>` subcommand that emits the script. Cost: ~30 LOC + 1 test. Out of stabilization scope (additive functionality); document as a v1.9 follow-up.

interaction_pain_points.example: fabric --help shows top-level command list. fabric init --help shows args from i18n. Help text is short — `cli.init.args.reapply.description` = 'Reapply Fabric-managed files and stage installers over an existing setup' (en.ts:94). It does NOT enumerate which files are reapplied, which are preserved, or warn about events.jsonl truncation. The reapply_semantics audit above is invisible to help readers.
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Expand --reapply help text to include: 'Overwrites: bootstrap README, agents.meta.json (to stub), INITIAL_TAXONOMY.md, forensic.json. TRUNCATES: events.jsonl. Preserves: .fabric/rules/, .fabric/init-context.json. Use `fab doctor --fix` after to rebuild the rule index.' Plus equivalent zh-CN. Cost: ~10 LOC i18n.

interaction_pain_points.example: packages/server/src/services/doctor.ts emits ONLY English messages. All 9 createXxxCheck functions use hardcoded English strings (L498, L505, L519, L532, L542, L569, L594, L605, L621). The CLI doctor wrapper at packages/cli/src/commands/doctor.ts:72-74 prints English section headers ('Fixable errors:', 'Manual errors:', 'Warnings:') hardcoded. Yet the rest of the CLI (init, scan, bootstrap, config) has full zh-CN/en i18n via t().
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Two-stage. (1) Quick fix in v1.7.1: translate the 3 doctor section headers in cli/commands/doctor.ts to use t(). Cost: ~10 LOC. (2) Full fix in v1.8.0: lift doctor messages from doctor.ts to i18n keys. Issue: doctor.ts is in @fenglimg/fabric-server which currently does NOT import @fenglimg/fabric-shared/i18n. Either route messages back to CLI for translation, OR add a translation layer accepting i18n locale via runDoctorReport options. Cost: ~80 LOC. Defer (b) to v1.8.0.

interaction_pain_points.example: fab serve prints `${symbol.ok} fabric serve http://host:port` ONLY (serve.ts:73). No info on what was loaded, no 'watching .fabric/agents.meta.json + bootstrap README for changes' note. Users debugging 'why is my edit not picked up?' have no signal.
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Add stderr lines: 'Watching: .fabric/agents.meta.json, .fabric/bootstrap/README.md' and 'Cache TTL: 5s'. Also note that .fabric/rules/ is NOT watched (current bug per rule_change_review pain points). Cost: ~10 LOC.

interaction_pain_points.example: packages/cli/src/commands/serve.ts:65-71 catches EADDRINUSE and re-throws a translated message suggesting 'try port + 1'. But other startHttpServer errors (e.g., chokidar watcher failure, AUTH_TOKEN required) are NOT caught and surface as raw stack traces. Domain.json already flagged signal-handler absence (R3); add to that the other startup error paths.
interaction_pain_points.fix_idea: Wrap startHttpServer in a try/catch that maps known error.code values to translated user-friendly messages (EACCES → 'permission denied for port <port>', ENOTFOUND → 'host unreachable'). Cost: ~30 LOC. Defer to v1.8.0 alongside R3.

stabilization_recommendations.title: Reclassify content_ref_missing as fixable_error and route through writeRuleMeta
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: Currently doctor reports content_ref_missing as manual_error even though doctor --fix's existing writeRuleMeta path would auto-recover (the missing-file's node would be dropped from the regenerated meta). Mislabel forces users to do manual work the system already automates. Pure issue-classification change in createRuleContentRefCheck (doctor.ts:574-581) plus add 'content_ref_missing' to the fix-routing condition at doctor.ts:271.
evidence_refs: packages/server/src/services/doctor.ts:270-282 (fix routing — would already work for content_ref_missing if added)

stabilization_recommendations.title: Add doctor warning rules_dir_unindexed for manually-added rules
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: Manual additions to .fabric/rules/ are silently invisible to fab_plan_context until doctor --fix runs. This is the #1 surprise for users who treat .fabric/rules/ like a normal directory. New doctor warning surfaces unindexed .md files; doctor --fix already regenerates correctly. Surface it.

stabilization_recommendations.title: Add doctor manual_error stable_id_collision for duplicate fab:rule-id declarations
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: Two rules with the same stable_id silently overwrite via Map.set last-wins (rule-meta-builder.ts:372-382). Downstream rule-test linkage is non-deterministic. Inspect duplicates inside inspectMeta (doctor.ts:322) using meta.nodes; fail with a manual_error code. Block doctor --fix until resolved (manual_errors take priority).

stabilization_recommendations.title: --reapply preserves events.jsonl AND skips agents.meta.json regen if rules exist
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: Two destructive surprises in --reapply that contradict the user's intent ('refresh, don't reset'). Fix in executeInitFabricPlan at init.ts:572-580: (a) if eventsPath exists, skip the rewrite (or rotate to events.jsonl.bak with one ledger entry 'init_reapplied'); (b) if .fabric/rules/ contains .md files, skip the agents.meta.json overwrite — leave it for the user to run doctor --fix. Both behaviors are conservative and recoverable.

stabilization_recommendations.title: i18n the 3 hardcoded section headers in CLI doctor wrapper
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: doctor.ts:72-74 hardcodes 'Fixable errors:', 'Manual errors:', 'Warnings:' — every other CLI command uses t(). zh-CN users see jarring mixed output. Quick fix in 1.7.1 (deprecation release) restores i18n surface uniformity.

stabilization_recommendations.title: Append actionable hints to all 9 doctor check messages
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: Every issueCheck() callsite emits a problem statement with no fix hint. First-time users hit init_context_missing or taxonomy_missing and get no clue what to do. Pattern: append '. Recommended: <command-or-skill-name>.' to every message. Pair with R-26 (server-side i18n) so Chinese users get equivalent hints.

stabilization_recommendations.title: Bring codex-skills/fabric-init/SKILL.md to feature parity with claude-skills/agents-md-init/SKILL.md
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: The Codex SKILL is dramatically thinner than the Claude SKILL — does not prescribe writing init-context.json, generating rules, or updating agents.meta.json. Codex users get a different (worse) handoff. Since 1.7.0 narrows scope to {Claude, Codex, Cursor}, parity is a brand-quality must-have. Replicate the 3-phase workflow in Codex's SKILL markdown.

stabilization_recommendations.title: Doctor warning rule_test_orphans_present when orphan_annotations.length > 0
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: @fabric-verify annotations whose stable_id no longer exists become orphan_annotations silently. Today they accumulate in rule-test.index.json with no doctor surface. Surfacing them as a warning gives users a signal that a rename / deletion broke a test linkage. Pure additive doctor check.

stabilization_recommendations.title: Doctor warning rule_declared_id_invalid when extractDeclaredStableId rejects a comment
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: If a user writes `<!-- fab:rule-id 1bad -->`, the regex rejects it silently and falls back to derived. User believes their declared id was honored. Surface the rejection.

stabilization_recommendations.title: Watch .fabric/rules/**/*.md in fab serve cacheWatcher
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: Currently chokidar at http.ts:167 watches only agents.meta.json + bootstrap README. Editing a rule file with serve running doesn't invalidate the cache. Extend the watch glob; on change, contextCache.invalidate('file_watch'). Note: this does NOT auto-rebuild meta — that requires doctor --fix. But it does ensure the next plan-context call re-hashes from disk.

stabilization_recommendations.title: init-context-missing message points users at the agents-md-init / fabric-init skill
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: Every fresh fab-init produces init_context_missing as a manual_error with message '.fabric/init-context.json is missing.' This is the #1 confusing post-init error because doctor --fix doesn't resolve it (only an AI skill can). Append to the message: 'Run the agents-md-init skill in Claude Code (or fabric-init in Codex) to generate it.' Tiny i18n change.

stabilization_recommendations.title: init scaffold detects pre-existing CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md / .cursorrules and warns
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: Migrating users with prior tooling end up with two parallel system-prompt sources (their root file + Fabric's MCP-served bootstrap). init silently ignores. Add a stderr warning during stage 1 listing detected files with text 'These pre-existing files are still consumed by your AI client; consider migrating their content to .fabric/rules/.' Pure additive — no behavior change.

stabilization_recommendations.title: Lift doctor messages to i18n catalog (full server-side i18n)
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: doctor.ts emits English-only because @fenglimg/fabric-server doesn't import @fenglimg/fabric-shared/i18n. Pair with R19 (action hints) to ship full doctor i18n. Defer past v1.8.0 — the fix has architectural implications (translation locale must be threaded through runDoctorReport options) and isn't pure stability.
evidence_refs: packages/cli/src/commands/doctor.ts (CLI is the i18n boundary today)

stabilization_recommendations.title: --reapply wizard step warns about destructive operations
stabilization_recommendations.rationale: Even after R17 makes --reapply less destructive, the wizard should explicitly call out which files will be touched. Adds a wizard step gated on options.reapply that lists the files and requires explicit confirmation (default=false, the only false-default in the wizard).

questions_for_user: R17 / --reapply semantics: option (a) preserve events.jsonl + don't touch agents.meta.json when rules/ has files; option (b) rotate events.jsonl to events.jsonl.bak and append a 'init_reapplied' ledger entry; option (c) status quo + bigger wizard warning. Recommendation: (a) — minimum surprise. Confirm direction before coding.

R20 (Codex skill parity): replicate the Claude SKILL.md content verbatim (translated UX-language to match Codex conventions), or take the opportunity to redesign both into a shared template with per-client overlays? Recommendation: verbatim parity now, redesign in a future cycle.

Cursor handoff gap (no skill, no hook in init scaffold): is this in-scope for v1.8.0? It's not strictly stability; it's first-class-citizen completeness for the kept-3 client set. Recommendation: defer to v1.9 unless we're treating Cursor parity as a brand quality must-have.

R26 (server-side i18n in doctor): the rough design is to thread `locale: 'en' | 'zh-CN'` through runDoctorReport options OR have the CLI re-translate keyed messages after reading the report. Which approach?

Should we add an explicit 'fab init --status' / 'fab status' command to surface the init-context-missing/handoff-incomplete state without running the full doctor sweep? This is a UX nicety not in stabilization scope, but the data exists in createInitContextCheck.

Doctor warning vs error semantics: R15 (rules_dir_unindexed) and R21 (rule_test_orphans_present) — should these be warnings (don't fail strict mode) or fixable_errors? Recommendation: warnings — they don't block correct operation, only surface drift signals.

_metadata.perspective_focus: interaction layer, init pipeline, rule-change review
_metadata.scan_strategy: Read prior 4 explorations + discussion timeline; targeted reads of rule-meta-builder.ts (797 lines, full), doctor.ts (relevant sections L1-625, L665-820), init.ts (key seams: cmd def, runInitCommand, buildInitFabricPlan, executeInitFabricPlan, executeInitStagePlan, wizard, taxonomy generators, --reapply path), serve.ts (full), scan.ts (full), bootstrap-guide.ts (full), http.ts (chokidar watcher block). Searched for init-context.json writers (none in cli, all in skill templates and external hooks). Confirmed Claude vs Codex skill parity gap by reading both SKILL.md files. Verified plan-context and get-rules iterate meta.nodes only.
key_implicit_dependencies: init-context.json: read by doctor (mandatory check), read by 3 hooks (Stop blockers), written by AI skills (Claude rich, Codex thin) — never written by CLI
agents.meta.json.nodes: drives fab_plan_context, get-rules iteration, doctor content_ref check — but rule files in .fabric/rules/ are only indexed via doctor --fix, not auto-discovered
stable_id (declared via fab:rule-id): consumed by @fabric-verify test annotations; collision/invalid silently degrade to derived stable_id; not watched/validated proactively
Stop hooks block AI completion until init-context.json exists — but the hook does not validate WHO writes the file or that it has the right schema, just existence
